title: Chvátal–Tuza Question on Maximum Induced Cycles
statement_text: What is the maximum possible number of induced cycles in a graph on $n$ vertices?
context_text: Chvátal and Tuza raised this problem in the 1980s (see references [15] and [16] of the paper). Robson showed independently in the 1980s that a graph on $n$ vertices has at most $3^{(1+o(1))n/3}$ induced cycles, but the exact maximum and the extremal graphs remained unknown.
paper_contribution: Proves that for all sufficiently large $n$, $H_n$ is the unique $n$-vertex graph achieving the maximum $m(n)$ induced cycles, and determines $m(n)$ exactly as a closed-form function of $n \bmod 3$ (Theorem 1.2 and Corollary 1.3).
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction, not in a labelled theorem environment. The abstract explicitly confirms this paper answers the question. Section 6 of the paper discusses further open questions but is not included in the provided text.
paper_title: Maximising the number of induced cycles in a graph

title: Chvátal–Tuza Conjecture on Maximum Odd Induced Cycles
statement_text: The maximum possible number of odd induced cycles in a graph on $n$ vertices is $3^{n/3}$.
context_text: Tuza conjectured this jointly with Chvátal in 1988 (see references [14], [15] and [16] of the paper) as a companion to their question on total induced cycles. The abstract of this paper states that this conjecture is resolved by the results herein.
paper_contribution: Proves Theorem 1.6: for all sufficiently large $n$, $G_n$ is the unique $n$-vertex graph containing $m_o(n)$ induced odd cycles, resolving the conjecture; $m_o(n)$ is within an $O(n)$ additive term of $3^{n/3}$ when $n \not\equiv 3 \pmod{6}$ and within a constant factor when $n \equiv 3 \pmod{6}$.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction, not in a labelled theorem environment. The abstract explicitly states this paper resolves the conjecture.
paper_title: Maximising the number of induced cycles in a graph
